angle: What it's like building a product while working full-time
threads: Building kakisewa while working full-time is basically: 8am–6pm doing my actual job, then 9pm–midnight arguing with myself about database schemas. Currently cold emailing 8,471 Malaysian rental agents at 100/day. Did I have time to build this? No. Did I do it anyway? Unfortunately yes.

slides: What building a product while working full-time actually looks like
Your 'deep work' hours are 10pm on a Tuesday
You ship features in 90-minute windows between meetings
Every weekend is a negotiation between rest and roadmap
Currently cold emailing 8,471 rental agents. 100 a day. One by one.
It's not romantic. It's just stubbornness with a Notion doc.
But it's the only way I know how to find out if this is real
carousel.caption: kakisewa is getting built in the gaps. Sharing the whole thing as it happens.

angle: Day 1 — Building in public: zero paying customers
caption:
I built a SaaS and have zero paying customers.

Here is what week 1 actually looked like.

What are you building right now? Drop it in the comments.
threads_text:
I built a SaaS and have zero paying customers.

Here is what week 1 actually looked like: the launch nobody saw, the Stripe dashboard I kept refreshing, and what building while employed actually costs you.

What are you building right now?

slides.main_text: I built a SaaS and have **zero paying customers.**
slides.sub_text: Here is what week 1 actually looked like.
After 7 days live. Fully launched. Real people able to sign up.
slides.bubble: I kept refreshing Stripe expecting something to happen. Nothing happened.

slides.main_text: I built it in **evenings and weekends.**
slides.sub_text: While working full-time at a US startup in Malaysia.
slides.sub_text_2: No co-founder. No investors. No announcement.
slides.bubble: My colleagues think I go to bed early.

slides.main_text: I launched **quietly.**
slides.sub_text: No Product Hunt. No X post. No fanfare.
slides.sub_text_2: Just pushed it live and waited to see if anyone noticed.
slides.bubble: The scariest part was not launching. It was nobody noticing.

slides.main_text: The gap between **it is ready** and anyone caring.
slides.sub_text: Nobody is waiting for your product. You have to go find them.
slides.bubble: Week 1 taught me: building is the easy part. Distribution is the job.

slides.main_text: Zero customers does not mean **zero progress.**
slides.sub_text: It means you are still early enough to get it right.
slides.follow_cta: Follow to know what happens next or save this for later.
slides.bubble: Month 2 starts tomorrow. Let's see if the emails start converting.

angle: Day 1 — Navigating career: left MBB after 5 years
caption:
I left a $60k/month job at MBB after 5 years. Probably one of the dumbest decisions I have ever made. Here is everything I did not expect.

Have you ever left a job that looked great from the outside? Drop it in the comments.
threads_text: I left a $60k/month job at MBB. Nobody told me what comes next.

slides.main_text: I left a **$60k/month job** at MBB after 5 years.
slides.sub_text: Probably one of the dumbest decisions I have ever made in my life.

slides.title: The Exit
slides.sub_text: Everyone asked why I was leaving. Two years more and I'll get promoted to leadership. It's a stupid decision.
slides.stat_label: years at MBB before I walked away
slides.bubble: Everyone asked why I was leaving but I kept asking myself why I was staying.

slides.title: The Identity
slides.main_text: I used to be **very proud** of my business cards.
slides.sub_text: Now they make me question my identity.
slides.bubble: I still have my old business cards. I keep them as a reminder, not a trophy.

slides.title: The Reality
slides.main_text: The meetings you **complained about** was also routine comfort.
slides.sub_text: The structure, the meetings, the drill, everything was so organized.
slides.bubble: Week one. No meetings, no deliverables, no one to report to. I did not know what to do with myself.

slides.title: The Loss
slides.main_text: Here is what nobody **puts on the list.**
slides.sub_text: The title. The peers. The salary. And knowing exactly what you were supposed to do every day.
slides.bubble: I feel disconnected. I feel like I lost a part of my identity. I don't know who I am.

slides.title: The Takeaway
slides.main_text: Leaving is not the **brave part.** Figuring out who you are after is.
slides.sub_text: Still working on it. I want to share to the world as it happens.

angle: Things I genuinely miss about consulting
caption:
Things I genuinely miss about consulting. Shorter list than I expected. More honest than most people admit.

What do you miss most about a job you left? Comment below.
threads_text: Things I genuinely miss about consulting. Shorter list than I expected.

slides.main_text: Things I genuinely miss about **consulting.**
slides.sub_text: Shorter list than I expected. More honest than most people admit.

slides.title: The Count
slides.sub_text: Things I actually miss. Not things I am supposed to say I miss.
slides.stat_label: things on the honest list. One of them is embarrassing.
slides.bubble: I miss the salary. Including that. Not going to pretend otherwise.

slides.title: The Variety
slides.main_text: A new **problem every 3 months.** That part, I miss.
slides.sub_text: Different industry every few months. No way to stay slow.
slides.bubble: I complained about the pace then. I would not now.

slides.title: The People
slides.main_text: The colleagues who **made you sharper.**
slides.sub_text: Being the least sharp person in the room every day does something to you. I did not expect to miss that pressure.
slides.bubble: I have not had a peer group like that since. That surprised me.

slides.title: The Salary
slides.main_text: I miss the **pay. A lot.** That is the embarrassing one.
slides.sub_text: Not just the pay but the lifestyle, meal budget, business travel, and 5 star hotels.
slides.bubble: I knew the number before I left. I still underestimated how much I would feel it.

slides.title: The Takeaway
slides.main_text: Missing something does not mean you were **wrong to leave.**
slides.sub_text: It means it was real. And real things leave a mark.

angle: Things I do not miss about consulting. This list is much longer.
caption:
Six things I do not miss about consulting. The list is longer but I had to stop somewhere.

(SOD = start of day, EOD = end of day alignment meetings. Daily check-ins to align on priorities and wrap up. Standard in consulting.)

What is the one thing you would never go back to from a previous job? Comment below.
threads_text:
Things I do not miss about consulting:

- performing busyness for the client
- solving problems that felt urgent and irrelevant a year later
- managing up instead of building anything
- travelling for a meeting that was definitely an email
- the firm name doing more work than I did
- timesheets

This list is longer. What would you never go back to?

slides.main_text:
Things I do not miss about consulting.
**This list is much longer.**
slides.sub_text: Six things, including one that sounds petty but is not.
I counted. Stopped at six because I have a day job.
slides.stat_label: things I do not miss. Honest count.
slides.bubble: The first week I did not have to submit a timesheet, I felt like I had been released from something.

slides.main_text: **Performing busyness** for the client
slides.sub_text: Looking occupied in a room is not the same as doing anything useful.
slides.sub_text_2: We were very good at looking occupied.
slides.bubble: Slide decks at 11pm that nobody asked for.

slides.main_text: Solving problems that felt **urgent in the room**
slides.sub_text: Revisit the recommendations a year later. Half of it did not matter.
slides.sub_text_2: The other half was already decided before we walked in.
slides.bubble: We called it impact. I'm not sure what it was.

slides.main_text: **Managing up** more than building anything
slides.sub_text: A lot of energy went into keeping stakeholders comfortable.
slides.sub_text_2: Very little went into making something that worked.
slides.bubble: I got good at it. That is the part that worries me.

slides.main_text: Travelling for work that was **an email**
slides.sub_text: Fly in Monday. Fly out Thursday. Repeat until your loyalty tier means something.
slides.bubble: The meeting could have been a Slack. The Slack could have been nothing.

slides.main_text: The **firm name** carrying your identity
slides.sub_text: You introduce yourself and people react to the logo, not to you.
slides.sub_text_2: Leaving tells you which one they were actually impressed by.
slides.bubble: Built kakisewa. Zero customers. That one is mine though.

slides.main_text: The petty one: **SOD and EOD alignment.**
slides.sub_text: We called it the manufacturing process. Makes you focus but also creates a false sense of urgency.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: For those who do not know: SOD = Start of Day, EOD = End of Day alignment. Daily check-ins every consultant has sat through.

angle: I took a significant pay cut to join a startup. Here are the honest numbers.
caption:
I left a job that pays RM55k/month to take a 40% pay cut and join a startup.

My fiancee never signed up for it. She started covering lunches, doing the groceries, swapping date nights out for staying in. Never once complained.

The upside is not in ringgit yet. It is the people around me now, and how much I am learning from them.

Is it worth it? I do not know. Do I regret it? At least not yet.

What is the biggest risk you have taken for work you actually wanted to do? Tell me below.
threads_text:
I left a job that pays RM55k/month. Took a 40% cut to join a startup.

The hours did not get lighter. The guilt (watching my fiancee quietly start covering lunches and groceries) got heavier.

But I am learning more from the founders around me than I ever did from consulting frameworks.

Is it worth it? I do not know. Do I regret it? At least not yet.

slides.main_text: I left a job that pays **RM55k/month**.
slides.sub_text: The honest numbers, and whether it was worth it.
My MBB base salary in Malaysia versus what I make now at Tarro
slides.stat_label: less than I made at MBB
slides.bubble: I told my parents the new number. They went quiet. Then they asked if the company had medical benefits.

slides.main_text: The **lifestyle change** hit me first.
slides.sub_text: Fewer dinners out. A longer pause before every non-essential purchase. Not dramatic on any single day. Just constant.
slides.bubble: I have started noticing when a restaurant has a cheaper lunch set. That is new behaviour for me.

slides.main_text: Then it hit **someone else too.**
slides.sub_text: My fiancee did not sign up for a pay cut. I did. She started paying for lunches, doing the groceries, swapping date nights out for staying in.
slides.bubble: She has never once complained about it. That somehow makes the guilt worse, not better.

slides.main_text: The **hours** did not get lighter.
slides.sub_text: Tarro by day, kakisewa by night. Same late finishes as MBB, just a different reason for them. I did not trade hard work for less hard work. I traded it for different work.
slides.bubble: Nobody tells you 'follow what you actually want' still means working past midnight. It just feels different at midnight.

slides.main_text: So why do it? **The people.**
slides.sub_text: I am surrounded by founders and operators who think completely differently from consultants. I learn something new almost every week just from being near them.
slides.bubble: At MBB I learned frameworks. Here I am learning how people actually build things from zero.

slides.main_text: It is **not paying off** yet. Not in ringgit.
slides.sub_text: Kakisewa has zero paying customers and a cold email list of 30,000 agents. The ROI is not mathing yet. I am still showing up anyway.
slides.bubble: 30,000 cold emails and zero replies that convert into paying customers. Humbling in a very specific way.

slides.main_text: **Is it worth it?** I do not know.
slides.sub_text: Do I regret it? At least not yet.

angle: First tool I have ever built, started because of my cousins pain point, not to make money
caption:
Still zero customers. But I did not start this to make money.

My cousin would not stop complaining about the same problem every time I saw him. I had never built a tool before in my life. I just wanted to see if I could.

The more I use it, the more holes I find. It is never going to be perfect. I am still glad I started.

Has someone else's pain point ever pushed you to build something? Tell me below.
threads_text:
Building in public: this is the first tool I have ever built. Not for the money, my cousin would not stop complaining about the same problem every time I saw him.

The more I use it, the more holes I find. It will never be perfect. Still glad I started.

slides.main_text: I am building a tool for the most **unsexy market** I could find.
slides.sub_text: Not because it makes money. Because my cousin would not stop complaining about the same problem.

slides.title: The starting point
slides.sub_text: No market research. No spreadsheet of competitors. Just one conversation that stuck with me.
slides.stat_label: tools I had built before this one. This is my first.
slides.bubble: This is tool number one. I am learning as I go.

slides.title: The real reason
slides.main_text: My cousin's **day to day pain point** is the whole reason this exists.
slides.sub_text: He was not pitching me an idea. He was just venting about the same thing, over and over, every time I saw him.
slides.bubble: He was not asking me to build anything. I could not stop thinking about it anyway.

slides.title: Why I am actually doing this
slides.main_text: **Not for the money.** I just wanted to try building something real.
slides.sub_text: I wanted to see if I could take an idea all the way to something someone could actually use, and learn everything in between.
slides.bubble: If this never makes a cent, I will still have learned how to build.

slides.title: The holes
slides.main_text: The more I use it myself, **the more holes I find.**
slides.sub_text: I was sure it was done. Then I opened it again and found three things wrong before breakfast.
slides.bubble: I keep finding rough edges I swore were not there yesterday.

slides.title: The truth
slides.main_text: It will **never be perfect.**
slides.sub_text: I used to think I would ship once it was finished. Turns out finished was never going to happen.
slides.bubble: Perfect was always just an excuse to keep it to myself a little longer.

slides.title: The takeaway
slides.main_text: **I am just glad I started.**
slides.sub_text: Zero paying customers, a dozen rough edges, and I would still do it again.
slides.bubble: Ask me again in six months. For now, I started. That is the whole win.

angle: Why consulting pays fresh grads RM22k a month, and why almost nobody gets in
caption:
Which job pays RM22,000 a month straight out of university?

Only 1 to 3 people get this offer per firm, per year. Some years, none.

Almost nobody has heard of the actual firms. That is exactly the point.

Swipe to see why it pays this much, and why almost nobody gets in.

Comment below what you want to know more about.
threads_text:
Which job pays RM22,000 a month straight out of university?

Only 1 to 3 people get this offer per firm, per year. Some years, none.

Almost nobody has heard of the actual firms. That is exactly the point.

Comment below what you want to know more about.

slides.main_text: Which job pays **RM22,000/month** straight out of university?
slides.sub_text: Most people have never heard of it. That is exactly the point.

slides.title: The odds
slides.sub_text: Thousands apply. Almost nobody gets in.
slides.stat_label: offers handed out per firm, per year. Some years, none.
slides.bubble: Not 1 to 3 percent. 1 to 3 people. Total.

slides.title: The firms
slides.main_text: They are the **Big 3** in consulting, MBB.
slides.sub_text: McKinsey, BCG, and Bain. If you have not heard of them, that is the point, they do not need to advertise.
slides.bubble: Everyone inside consulting knows these three names. Almost nobody outside does.

slides.title: Why it pays so much
slides.main_text: They work on **regional projects**, billed in USD and SGD.
slides.sub_text: That is the premium you are actually paying for, not local Malaysian rates.
slides.bubble: Same associate, same hours. Different currency, different paycheck.

slides.title: The prep
slides.main_text: Some students start prepping **in year one** of university.
slides.sub_text: Even then, about 0.05% of applicants get an offer.
slides.bubble: Three years of prep for a 45 minute interview.

slides.title: About that skepticism
slides.main_text: You do not have to believe the pay is real.
slides.sub_text: But once you see the odds of getting the job at all, it starts to make sense.
slides.bubble: A few of you did not believe RM55,000 a month either. Fair. Now you know why it is possible.

slides.title: Tell me
slides.main_text: **What do you want to know more about?**
slides.sub_text: 1. How to actually get into consulting. 2. What consulting work is really like. 3. Why I left despite the pay.
slides.bubble: Reply with a number. I will write about whichever one wins.

angle: AI infra stocks just dropped hard. My actual reaction, not a hot take.
caption:
Investors lost 20% to 30% in the stock market this past week. Here's why.

Led by AI infrastructure. Intel down 21%, Micron down 22%.

What I'm actually doing about it, including the two tickers I'm DCA-ing into right now.

What do you want to know more about?
(1) How I actually DCA into this
(2) Why supply constraints matter more than sentiment
(3) What I'm watching before TSMC earnings

Comment a number below.
threads_text:
Investors lost 20% to 30% in the stock market this past week. Here's why.

Led by AI infrastructure. Intel down 21%, Micron down 22%.

What I'm doing: DCA-ing into NVDA and TSM.

Want to know more?
(1) How I actually DCA
(2) Why supply constraints matter more than sentiment
(3) What I'm watching before TSMC earnings

Reply with a number.

slides.main_text: Investors lost **20% to 30%** in the stock market this past week.
slides.sub_text: Here's why.

slides.title: Led by AI infrastructure
slides.sub_text: Intel led the slide
slides.bubble: This is not one bad earnings report. This is a whole sector repricing at once.

slides.title: What is going on
slides.main_text: It started with **Samsung earnings**, then spread fast.
slides.sub_text: SK Hynix slowing HBM expansion. A China AI chip. A hawkish Fed.
slides.sub_text_2: The S&P 500 and Nasdaq are down from last month's highs too.
slides.bubble: When the whole market wobbles, not just one sector, that changes how I read it.

slides.title: My actual move
slides.main_text: What I am doing: **dollar cost averaging into NVDA and TSM.**
slides.sub_text: Not because I know where the bottom is. I do not, and I am not pretending to.
slides.bubble: TSMC reports earnings July 16. That is the next real signal I am watching, not another hot take on Threads.

slides.title: The 1 to 2 year view
slides.main_text: Zoom out **1 to 2 years** and the picture changes.
slides.sub_text: The chips this boom depends on are still supply constrained. That does not change in a week.
slides.bubble: Short term, this can move either way. That constraint is the part I actually have conviction on.

slides.title: Tell me
slides.main_text: **What do you want to know more about?**
slides.sub_text: 1. How I actually DCA into this. 2. Why supply constraints matter more than sentiment. 3. What I am watching before TSMC earnings.
slides.bubble: Reply with a number. I will write about whichever one wins.

angle: I wasted my first month building with Claude Code. Every mistake, honestly.
caption:
I wasted my first month building with Claude Code. Prompts like 'build me a $1M MRR tool' and wondering why nothing worked.

Turns out the tool was never the problem. I was.

Sharing the actual fixes next, taught to me by my friend Ethan.
threads_text:
I wasted my first month building with Claude Code. Every mistake, honestly.

"Build me a $1M MRR tool." My all time favorite prompt. Never worked.

Spent 50% of my tokens on one UI bug. For weeks.

Then I saw what my friend built with it. Turns out it was never Claude. It was me.

Next post: 2 things that made Claude Code my best friend, taught to me by my friend Ethan.

slides.main_text: I wasted my **first month** building with Claude Code. Every mistake, honestly.
slides.sub_text: Vibe coding sounds easy until you actually try it.

slides.title: The most honest prompt I ever wrote
slides.main_text: "Why is this so **ugly**. Fix it. idk what is wrong but **fix it.**"
slides.sub_text: An actual prompt I sent. More than once.
slides.bubble: Nothing worked. Because I never told it what was actually wrong.

slides.title: Claude is not invincible
slides.main_text: If you do not understand what you are building, **Claude does not either.**
slides.sub_text: I kept nodding along at code I did not understand.
slides.bubble: Asking Claude to build the best UI doesn't help, unfortunately.

slides.title: Guilty as charged
slides.main_text: "**Build me a $1M MRR tool.**"
slides.sub_text: My all time favorite prompt. Except it never worked.
slides.bubble: I am sure I am not the only one who has tried this.

slides.title: The bug that ate weeks
slides.main_text: I spent **50% of my tokens** in month one on one UI bug.
slides.sub_text: Prompted over and over again. For weeks.
slides.bubble: "Please make sure the cardboard doesn't move with other components." Times 26.

slides.title: It was never Claude
slides.main_text: I thought **Claude Code was bad.**
slides.sub_text: Then I saw what my friend built with it. Awesome UI. Fully functional. Something I would actually pay for.
slides.bubble: It clearly was not Claude. It was me.

slides.title: What comes next
slides.main_text: I will share **2 things** that made Claude Code my best friend.
slides.sub_text: Both taught to me by my friend, Ethan.
slides.follow_cta: Follow along for more like this.
slides.bubble: Wish I had known these on day one.

angle: The real setup behind Claude Code: MCP, Playwright, shadcn, 21st.dev, and Context7, explained in plain terms.
caption:
I spent too much time prompting a bad tool.

MCP is the plug that lets Claude reach real tools, like a USB port for AI. Playwright clicks through a real browser like a person. Shadcn and 21st.dev build the interface. Context7 keeps the docs current.

4 skills later, grateful for all of them.

Which one would you use first?
threads_text:
I spent too much time prompting a bad tool.

MCP is the plug that lets Claude reach real tools, like a USB port for AI. Playwright clicks through a real browser like a person. Shadcn and 21st.dev build the interface. Context7 keeps the docs current.

4 skills later, grateful for all of them.

Which one would you use first?

slides.main_text: I spent **too much time** prompting a bad tool.
slides.sub_text: Here is the setup that actually changed how I build.

slides.title: Four
slides.sub_text: The tools covered in this post.
slides.stat_label: skills that changed how I build
slides.bubble: Each one replaces hours of prompting a tool that was not built for this.

slides.title: What MCP actually is
slides.main_text: **MCP** is the plug that lets Claude reach real tools.
slides.sub_text: Think of it like a USB port for AI. One standard plug, any tool on the other end, instead of a custom setup for every single one.
slides.bubble: This is the difference between an assistant that talks and one that actually does the work.

slides.title: What Playwright does
slides.main_text: **Playwright** lets Claude open a real browser.
slides.sub_text: It clicks buttons, fills forms, resizes the screen, and takes screenshots, exactly like a real visitor would.
slides.bubble: It once caught a button rendering invisible behind another element. I would never have found that just reading code.

slides.title: Where the UI comes from
slides.main_text: **Shadcn** and **21st.dev** build the interface.
slides.sub_text: Shadcn gives Claude ready made, accessible components it can copy straight into the project, not install as a dependency. 21st.dev generates new ones from a plain text description.
slides.bubble: I do not design a single button myself anymore.

slides.title: Context7 keeps Claude current
slides.main_text: **Context7** stops Claude from guessing outdated docs.
slides.sub_text: It pulls the real, current documentation for whatever tool I am using, instead of relying on what Claude was trained on months ago.
slides.bubble: AI tools change every week. This is how Claude keeps up instead of falling behind.

slides.title: More coming
slides.main_text: There are **more skills** I have not covered yet.
slides.sub_text: I will go through the rest next.
slides.follow_cta: Follow along for the rest of the toolkit.
slides.bubble: Still building the list as I go.

angle: I left the best job title I will probably ever have. Here is what nobody tells you comes next.
caption:
I walked away from a job title that used to speak for me.

Most people warned me about the pay cut. Nobody warned me about the identity part.

What happens when your entire personality was "consultant at [firm]" and then suddenly it isn't?

Have you ever left a prestigious job for something uncertain? What pushed you?
threads_text:
I walked away from a job title that used to speak for me.

Everyone warned me about the pay cut. Nobody warned me half my personality was just the firm name.

Now when people ask what I do, I have no clean answer. Nobody prepares you for that.

Ever left a prestigious job for something unknown? What pushed you?

slides.main_text: I walked away from a **job title** that used to speak for me.
slides.sub_text: The identity crisis nobody warns you about when you leave a prestigious job for something unknown.
Years I spent in consulting before I walked away from it
slides.bubble: Long enough for it to become who I was. Short enough that people still ask why I left.

slides.main_text: The **prestige was real.** So was the trap.
slides.sub_text: The brand opened doors. It also became a crutch, until I stopped knowing if I was good or just well-labeled.
slides.bubble: The firm name was doing a lot of heavy lifting in conversations I didn't notice.

slides.main_text: Nobody outside consulting **cares what tier your firm was.**
slides.sub_text: Inside the bubble it's everything. Outside it's a blank stare.
slides.bubble: First time I said the firm name to a non-corporate person and got nothing back. Humbling.

slides.main_text: Going from **"consultant at X"** to not having a title at all is a full identity reset.
slides.sub_text: The old answer to "what do you do" was a conversation starter. The new one is a conversation ender.
slides.bubble: I still fumble the answer when people ask what I do now. There isn't a clean one anymore.

slides.main_text: I expected **freedom, clarity, momentum.**
slides.sub_text: It's a job, a side project with no customers yet, and a lot of quiet. Clarity comes eventually, just not with the resignation letter.
slides.bubble: I genuinely thought leaving would feel like a movie moment. It felt more like a Sunday with no plans.

slides.main_text: The title was never the thing. **You learn that after you give it up.**
slides.sub_text: I still have my old business cards. I keep them as a reminder, not a trophy.
slides.follow_cta: Have you ever left a prestigious job for something uncertain? What pushed you?
slides.bubble: Not having an answer for "what do you do" is humbling in ways no consulting project ever was.

angle: I thought I was investing. I was just buying companies I could explain at dinner parties.
caption: Three years of investing before I realised I was just buying companies I could explain at dinner parties. What was the first thing you ever invested in? Drop it below — genuinely curious, zero judgment.
threads_text: I thought I was investing. I was just buying companies I could explain at dinner parties. Knew exactly what they did. Had no idea if they were mispriced. Three years and average returns to learn the difference. What was the first stock or asset you ever bought?

slides.main_text: I thought I was investing. **I was just buying companies I could explain at dinner parties.**
slides.sub_text: Three years and average returns to figure out the difference.
How long it took me to understand what an edge actually is
slides.stat_label: years investing before I got this
slides.bubble: My portfolio looked like a TED Talk watchlist. Great companies. Average returns.

slides.main_text: **Knowing what a company does** is not the same as knowing it is mispriced.
slides.sub_text: I could explain the business model in two sentences. That felt like research. It was not research.
slides.bubble: Turns out everyone at the dinner party already knew too.

slides.main_text: The **clarity trap**: understanding a company does not equal conviction it will outperform.
slides.sub_text: A good business and a good investment are different questions. I kept confusing the first for the second.
slides.bubble: Bought Apple at a price that made complete sense and still underperformed the index.

slides.main_text: I was buying **stories**, not bets.
slides.sub_text: Stories feel safe. Bets require you to be specifically right about something the market is specifically wrong about.
slides.bubble: Much easier to say 'I believe in this company' than to say exactly what you think the market is missing.

slides.main_text: The question I should have been asking: **what do I believe that the market does not?**
slides.sub_text: If the answer is nothing, you are not making a bet. You are just paying market price for market returns.
slides.bubble: Still working on having a real answer to this. Most days I don't.

slides.main_text: Liking a company and **having an edge on a company** are not the same thing.
slides.sub_text: What was the first thing you ever invested in? Drop it below.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Mine was a Malaysian bank stock I bought because I had an account there. True story.

angle: Building as a non-technical person is hell mode. Here is what makes it easier.
caption:
Building as a non-technical person is hell mode. Here's the one habit that helps.

Never start with build. Explore first, real references and best practices. Then Plan, let Claude lay it out. Then Execute.

Skipping straight to build is how you end up debugging code you don't understand.

Do you plan before you build? Vote: always / sometimes / never
threads_text:
Building as a non-technical person is hell mode. One habit made it easier: never start with build.

Explore first, then Plan, then Execute. Skipping straight to code is how you end up debugging something you don't understand.

Do you plan before you build, or just wing it?

slides.main_text: Building as a non-technical person is hell mode. **One habit** made it survivable.
slides.sub_text: Not a tool. A sequence.
Steps, every single time, no matter how small the task
slides.bubble: Skipping straight to build is how I've wasted the most hours, by far.

slides.main_text: **Explore first.** Real references, real best practices.
slides.sub_text: If I do not understand it, Claude does not either. I ask it to explain the codebase back to me before anything gets touched.
slides.bubble: Feels slow. Saves hours later.

slides.main_text: **Then Plan.** Let Claude lay it all out first.
slides.sub_text: I read the plan like a spec, not a suggestion.
slides.bubble: Half my edits happen here, before a single line is written.

slides.main_text: **Then Execute.** Only after the first two.
slides.sub_text: By the time we get here, there are barely any surprises left.
slides.bubble: This is the boring part now. That is the whole point.

slides.main_text: **Explore. Plan. Execute.** In that order, every time.
slides.sub_text: Skipping straight to build is still the mistake I have to actively resist.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Do you plan before you build, or just wing it?

angle: MBB taught me one skill that transfers to startups. Everything else I had to unlearn.
caption:
MBB taught me one skill that actually transfers to startups. The rest I had to unlearn, and some of it took embarrassingly long.

Swipe to see what stuck and what didn't.

What's the one skill from your current job that would help in a startup?
threads_text:
One skill from consulting transfers to startups. Everything else I had to unlearn.

The dangerous one: mistaking analysis for action. I was very good at being thorough. Startups don't reward thorough. They reward moving.

What's the one skill from your job that would actually help in a startup?

slides.main_text: MBB taught me **one skill** that transfers to startups.
slides.sub_text: Everything else I had to unlearn, and some of it took longer than I expected.
Number of skills from 5 years of consulting I use every single day building this
slides.stat_label: just one skill made the cut
slides.bubble: I thought it would be more. It is not more.

slides.main_text: **Structuring the problem** breaks into three things.
slides.sub_text: Ask the right question. Set the right goal. Get the right capability. I used to skip straight to solving.
slides.bubble: I spent years being the person with the smart-sounding answer. Still unlearning that.

slides.main_text: First: **asking the right question.**
slides.sub_text: A right question is specific enough to be wrong. Not "how do we grow," but "why did the last 10 people say no."
slides.bubble: I used to ask questions that sounded smart in a room and meant nothing once I sat down alone to actually build.

slides.main_text: Second: **setting the right goal.**
slides.sub_text: A right goal is a number you can hit or miss by Friday, not a five-year vision. Consulting trained me to set goals that sound good in a deck.
slides.bubble: My first goal for this was "grow steadily." That is not a goal. That is a wish.

slides.main_text: Third: **getting the right capability.**
slides.sub_text: In plain terms: do you actually have someone who can do the thing, not someone who can explain why it's hard.
slides.bubble: I cannot design, cannot really code, cannot sell yet. Some days that gap feels bigger than the idea itself.

slides.main_text: The dangerous habit: **mistaking analysis for action**
slides.sub_text: Consulting pays you to be right, not fast. Startups reward people who move; being thorough while the runway burns is just expensive thinking.
slides.bubble: I have 8,471 agents scraped and zero paying customers. Analysis did not cause that. But it did not help either.

slides.main_text: **One skill transfers. The rest is dead weight.**
slides.sub_text: Figure out which habits your current job is rewarding that a startup would punish.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Still working on this myself. 100 cold emails a day right now, hoping structure beats inertia.

angle: After trying everything, I use one investing principle. It is boring. It works.
caption: I don't DCA into an index fund — I bet on sectors right before they bottleneck: space, AI infra, semiconductors, biomed. Concentrated, not diversified, and I don't always time it right. What's your actual investing style? Vote: index funds / sector bets / individual stocks / not investing yet
threads_text: I don't dollar-cost-average into an index fund. I bet on sectors right before they bottleneck — space, AI infrastructure, semiconductors, biomed. Concentrated, not diversified. Higher risk, and I don't always get the timing right. What's your actual investing style?

slides.main_text: I don't dollar-cost-average into an index fund. **I bet on sectors right before they bottleneck.**
slides.sub_text: Space, AI infrastructure, semiconductors, biomed. Concentrated, not diversified.
Sectors I actually invest in, not the whole market
slides.bubble: I have strong opinions about supply chains most people have never thought about once.

slides.main_text: The thesis: **find the bottleneck before everyone else does.**
slides.sub_text: Every high-growth sector eventually hits a capacity constraint. That is usually where the value sits.
slides.bubble: Sounds smart until you are three months early and the position is just red.

slides.main_text: **Concentrated,** not diversified.
slides.sub_text: Four sectors, not four hundred stocks. I would rather be wrong with conviction than right by accident.
slides.bubble: My portfolio would give a financial advisor a small heart attack.

slides.main_text: This is **not** the boring, safe version of investing.
slides.sub_text: Higher risk, higher variance, and I do not always get the timing right.
slides.bubble: I have eaten some real losses being early. That part does not make it into the highlight reel.

slides.main_text: **Conviction, not comfort.** That is the actual strategy.
slides.sub_text: It will not work for everyone. It is the only version of investing I have ever been able to stick with.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Ask me again in ten years whether this was smart or just lucky.

angle: I have been building alone for months and nobody warned me about this part.
caption: Building alone is fine until it isn't. Solo founders — what is the one thing nobody warned you about? Drop it below.
threads_text: Nobody talks about this part of building alone. Not the workload. The silence. You make a call, sit with it, and there is literally no one to tell you if you got it wrong. Shipping solo with a full-time job and zero paying customers is a very specific kind of quiet. What is the hardest part nobody mentions?

slides.main_text: I have been building alone for months and **nobody warned me about this part.**
slides.sub_text: It is not the work. It is the silence.
People on the team who can tell you if you are making the right call.
slides.bubble: I texted my friend about a product decision at 11pm. He replied 'sounds good bro.' That was my feedback loop.

slides.main_text: There is **no standup. No Slack. No one to share a small win with.**
slides.sub_text: You hit a milestone and just close the laptop. The win still happened, it just evaporated.
slides.bubble: I told a friend. He said 'sounds good bro' and moved on.

slides.main_text: I made a **decision I am still not sure about.** Had to live with it alone.
slides.sub_text: No one to stress-test it. No one to talk me out of it or into it.
slides.bubble: I did eventually make a call. It might be wrong. I will probably find out in six weeks when the numbers tell me.

slides.main_text: Loneliness is not really about **people.** It is about **no one catching your blind spots.**
slides.sub_text: You can be surrounded and still building in a fog.
slides.bubble: A co-founder would push back. A friend replies 'sounds good bro.' These are not the same thing and I keep pretending they are.

slides.main_text: **Therapy is cheaper than a co-founder.** Barely.
slides.sub_text: At least someone is professionally obligated to engage with your decisions.
slides.bubble: Half joking. Maybe less than half.

slides.main_text: Building alone does not cost you work. It costs you **blind spots.**
slides.sub_text: Fill in the blank: nobody warned me building alone would ___.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Still figuring this one out. No clean lesson here.

angle: Consulting trained me to be excellent at things that do not matter. Startups cured me of this. Slowly.
caption:
MBB trained me to make things look good. Startups trained me to make things work. These are not the same skill.

I spent years getting very good at the wrong things. The transition was humbling.

What is something you optimised perfectly that turned out not to matter?
threads_text:
MBB trained me to make things look good. Startups trained me to make things work. These are not the same skill.

I spent years getting good at the wrong things.

What is something you optimised perfectly that turned out not to matter?

slides.main_text: Consulting trained me to be **excellent at things that do not matter.**
slides.sub_text: Startups cured me of this. Slower than I expected.
Hours I spent on slide decks that changed nothing at the client
slides.stat_label: hours on slides, 80% of everything I did for 5 years
slides.bubble: That is not an exaggeration. I did the math and immediately regretted it.

slides.main_text: The client decided in **10 minutes.** The deck took 3 weeks.
slides.sub_text: Pixel-perfect transitions, consistent font hierarchy, an appendix nobody opened. The decision was made before we even walked in.
slides.bubble: I once spent two days aligning boxes on a slide. Two days.

slides.main_text: The **consultant trap:** effort feels like impact because it looks like impact.
slides.sub_text: Hours in, polished output, rigorous process. But output is not outcome. Consulting rewards performing work; startups punish it.
slides.bubble: Nobody claps for your framework if the thing does not ship.

slides.main_text: A **working feature** beats a perfect plan. Every single time.
slides.sub_text: It is live. Zero paying customers, two working features. More real than any deck I ever made.
slides.bubble: My first pitch deck had 14 slides. what I built had 2 working features. I had the order wrong.

slides.main_text: The moment I realised I was **optimising for the wrong thing.**
slides.sub_text: I caught myself rewriting copy on a landing page nobody had visited. Classic consultant move: polish the thing, avoid the scary one.
slides.bubble: The scary thing was sending the cold email. The copy was fine.

slides.main_text: **Excellent execution** on the wrong thing is still the wrong thing.
slides.sub_text: The skill is not doing things well. It is picking the right things to do at all.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: What did you optimise perfectly that turned out not to matter?

angle: The job does not stop. Here is what I actually do to switch my brain off. It is not much.
caption: The job doesn't stop. My actual off switch: Haikyuu, Terrace House, and snacks from 9am to 3am straight. No meditation, no journaling, no walks. What's your real off switch, not the one you'd post about?
threads_text: The job doesn't stop. So my actual switch-off is Haikyuu and Terrace House marathons, 9am to 3am straight, snacking the whole time at home. No meditation, no journaling, no walks. What's your real off switch?

slides.main_text: The job does not stop. So I binge **Haikyuu** until 3am and call it switching off.
slides.sub_text: Not a wellness routine. Just anime, reality TV, and snacks.
How long a binge day actually runs, start to finish
slides.stat_label: hours straight, one sitting, more than once
slides.bubble: 9am to 3am. I have done the math. It is not a great number.

slides.main_text: No meditation. No journaling. No **walks.**
slides.sub_text: Just Haikyuu, Terrace House, and whatever else is queued up.
slides.bubble: I have tried to make this sound healthier for this post. I am not going to.

slides.main_text: The appeal is **zero decisions required.**
slides.sub_text: Everything else in my day needs a choice. This does not.
slides.bubble: My brain gets a day off from being the one in charge.

slides.main_text: This is not a **hack.** It is just what actually happens.
slides.sub_text: No app, no system, no optimisation. Just the couch and snacks from the kitchen.
slides.bubble: I am not proud of it. I am also not going to pretend it does not work.

slides.main_text: Switching off does not have to look **impressive.**
slides.sub_text: Mine looks like volleyball anime and instant noodles until 3am.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: What is your actual off switch, the real one, not the one you would post about?
